# Generate sample data from named distributions

`generate_distribution()` is a wrapper function for generating sample
data from named continuous distributions.

The distributional shapes include beta, Cauchy, chi-squared,
exponential, F, gamma, Laplace, logistic, log normal, normal, t,
triangular, uniform, and Weibull. See additional details below for
distribution parameters and default values.

## Usage

``` r
generate_distribution(
  distribution = c("beta", "cauchy", "chi-squared", "exponential", "f", "gamma",
    "laplace", "logistic", "log normal", "normal", "t", "triangular", "uniform",
    "weibull"),
  n = 1000,
  seed = NULL,
  alpha_shape = NULL,
  beta_shape = NULL,
  location = NULL,
  scale = NULL,
  df = NULL,
  df2 = NULL,
  rate = NULL,
  mean_log = NULL,
  sd_log = NULL,
  mean = NULL,
  sd = NULL,
  theta = NULL,
  min = NULL,
  max = NULL,
  summary = TRUE
)
```

## Arguments

- distribution:

  A character vector to identify which distributional shape to sample.
  Options include `beta,` `cauchy,` `chi-squared,` `exponential,` `f,`
  `gamma,` `laplace,` `logistic,` `log normal,` `normal,` `t,`
  `triangular,` `uniform,` and `weibull.`

- n:

  A numeric vector for the sample size. The default value is 1000.

- seed:

  An optional numeric vector to use in set.seed()

- alpha_shape:

  A non-negative numeric vector required for the shape of the beta,
  gamma, and Weibull distributions.

- beta_shape:

  A non-negative numeric vector required for the shape of the beta
  distribution.

- location:

  A numeric vector for the location of the Cauchy, Laplace, and logistic
  distributions.

- scale:

  A numeric vector for the scale of the Cauchy, Laplace, logistic, and
  Weibull distributions.

- df:

  A numeric vector required for the degrees of freedom of the
  chi-squared, F, and t distributions.

- df2:

  A numeric vector required for the degrees of freedom of the F
  distribution.

- rate:

  A numeric vector for the rate of the exponential and gamma
  distributions.

- mean_log:

  A numeric vector for the mean (on the log scale) of the log normal
  distribution.

- sd_log:

  A numeric vector for the standard deviation (on the log scale) of the
  log normal distribution.

- mean:

  A numeric vector for the mean of the normal distribution.

- sd:

  A numeric vector for the standard deviation of the normal
  distribution.

- theta:

  A numeric vector required for the theta of the triangular
  distribution.

- min:

  A numeric vector for the minimum value of the triangular and uniform
  distributions.

- max:

  A numeric vector for the maximum value of the triangular and uniform
  distributions.

- summary:

  A logical vector for whether the return should include summary
  statistics. The default value is TRUE.

## Value

A list of

- `data`, a vector of length n containing the generated values

- `summary`, a descriptive matrix of input and sample values of the
  specified distribution's parameters

## Distributional Shapes & Default Values

The **beta** distribution requires the alpha and beta shape parameters.
There are no default values for this distribution. See
[`stats::rbeta()`](https://rdrr.io/r/stats/Beta.html) for more
information.

The **Cauchy** distribution uses the location and scale parameters. The
default values are location = 0 and scale = 1. See
[`stats::rcauchy()`](https://rdrr.io/r/stats/Cauchy.html) for more
information.

The **chi-squared** distribution requires the df parameter. There is no
default value for this distribution. See
[`stats::rchisq()`](https://rdrr.io/r/stats/Chisquare.html) for more
information.

The **exponential** distribution uses the rate parameter, which has a
default value of 1. See
[`stats::rexp()`](https://rdrr.io/r/stats/Exponential.html) for more
information.

The **F** distribution requires the df and df2 parameters. There are no
default values for this distribution. See
[`stats::rf()`](https://rdrr.io/r/stats/Fdist.html) for more
information.

The **gamma** distribution requires the alpha shape and rate parameter.
There is no default value for alpha shape, but the default value for
rate is 1. See
[`stats::rgamma()`](https://rdrr.io/r/stats/GammaDist.html) for more
information.

The **Laplace** distribution uses the location and scale parameters. The
default values are location = 0 and scale = 1. See
[`VGAM::rlaplace()`](https://rdrr.io/pkg/VGAM/man/laplaceUC.html) for
more information.

The **logistic** distribution uses the location and scale parameters.
The default values are location = 0 and scale = 1. See
[`stats::rlogis()`](https://rdrr.io/r/stats/Logistic.html) for more
information.

The **log normal** distribution uses the mean log and sd log parameters.
The default values are mean log = 0 and sd log = 1. See
[`stats::rlnorm()`](https://rdrr.io/r/stats/Lognormal.html) for more
information.

The **normal** distribution uses the mean and sd parameters. The default
values are mean = 0 and sd = 1. See
[`stats::rnorm()`](https://rdrr.io/r/stats/Normal.html) for more
information.

The **t** distribution requires the df parameter. There is no default
value for this distribution. See
[`stats::rt()`](https://rdrr.io/r/stats/TDist.html) for more
information.

The **triangular** distribution requires the theta, min, and max
parameters. There is no default value for theta, but the other default
values are min = 0 and max = 1. See
[`VGAM::rtriangle()`](https://rdrr.io/pkg/VGAM/man/triangleUC.html) for
more information.

The **uniform** distribution uses the min and max parameters. The
default values are min = 0 and max = 1. See
[`stats::runif()`](https://rdrr.io/r/stats/Uniform.html) for more
information.

The **Weibull** distribution requires the alpha shape and scale
parameter. There is no default value for alpha shape, but the default
value for scale is 1. See
[`stats::rweibull()`](https://rdrr.io/r/stats/Weibull.html) for more
information.

## Examples

generate_distribution(distribution = "laplace")
